narrative: As a Black male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and backgrounds to forecast first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, LSAT score of 27.30, Year 1 school decile ranking of 6th (slightly above average), Year 3 decile of 2nd (well below average), middle family income bracket, and full-time student status—and predicted a 40% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This elevated risk threshold led to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar to maintain strong outcomes. The three most influential factors in this decision were your Year 3 academic decile ranking, your Year 1 decile ranking, and your undergraduate GPA, each pulling the prediction in different directions based on how they compared to patterns in past students. Most critically, your low 2nd-decile ranking in Year 3 grades—far below the typical 5th or 6th decile—strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure, as it signals a significant drop-off in performance that often correlates with exam struggles for students in similar situations. On a more positive note, your solid 6th-decile Year 1 ranking, just above average, worked against failure by suggesting early promise that many successful bar takers share. Similarly, your undergraduate GPA of 3.20, right around the dataset average of 3.21, provided a steadying influence toward passing, aligning with the profiles of students who typically succeed. Compared to typical admitted students, your LSAT score of 27.30 stands out as notably lower than the average 36.70, though it wasn't the top driver here, and your middle-income background and full-time status are common and neutral in the data. While these results are disappointing, especially given the challenges many Black male students face in competitive academic pipelines, they highlight addressable areas like later-year consistency. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your later undergraduate or graduate coursework to improve decile rankings, and seeking targeted bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students. Reapplying after these steps could shift your predicted odds favorably, and many in your position have succeeded by focusing on these levers. Your early strengths show potential—building on them strategically will make a real difference.
